title: Grafana Critical RCE via SQL Expressions CVE-2026-27876 (CVSS 9.1): Viewer-Level Users Exploit Arbitrary File Write to Overwrite Plugin Drivers and Establish SSH Backdoor on Host — Monitoring Infrastructure at Risk, Patches Released March 27

sources.name: Grafana Blog - Security Release Critical and High Severity Fixes CVE-2026-27876 and CVE-2026-27880
CyCognito - Emerging Threat Analysis CVE-2026-27876 Grafana RCE via SQL Expressions
Security Boulevard - Critical Grafana Vulnerabilities Enable Remote Code Execution and DoS Attacks
SecurityOnline - Grafana Critical RCE Vulnerability CVE-2026-27876 SQL Expressions
GBHackers - Critical Grafana Flaws Enable Remote Code Execution
CybersecurityNews - Grafana Vulnerabilities Enable Remote Code Execution
TheHackerWire - Grafana Critical RCE via SQL Expressions and Plugin Chaining
Cibersafety - CVE-2026-27876 Grafana Remote Code Execution Analysis
OffSeq Threat Radar - CVE-2026-27876 Grafana Vulnerability Intelligence

timeline.event: CVE-2026-27876 reserved; vulnerability discovered by Liad Eliyahu (Head of Research, Miggo Security) and reported to Grafana Labs via responsible disclosure bug bounty program
Grafana Enterprise customers receive private security patches under coordinated embargo, approximately two weeks ahead of public disclosure
Grafana Labs publishes security advisory and releases patches for all supported release lines: 11.6.14, 12.1.10, 12.2.8, 12.3.6, 12.4.2, and 13.0.0
Security researchers begin publishing technical analysis of the SQL Expressions arbitrary file write chain and its plugin-chaining exploitation path
Broader security media coverage begins; Linux distribution security teams publish advisories; patching urgency increases
No CISA KEV addition or confirmed active exploitation reported; significant number of Grafana instances remain unpatched

simple_summary.what_happened: A critical security vulnerability was discovered in Grafana — the world's most widely deployed open-source monitoring dashboard platform, used by millions of organizations to visualize their infrastructure metrics. Security researcher Liad Eliyahu at Miggo Security found that Grafana's 'SQL Expressions' feature — designed to let users write SQL-like queries to transform monitoring data — can be abused to write arbitrary files to any location on the Grafana server. By chaining this file write with Grafana Enterprise plugins (such as the Sqlyze database driver), an attacker holding only a low-privilege viewer account can overwrite driver configuration files to execute code on the underlying host server and install an SSH backdoor, achieving complete system compromise.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Organizations running Grafana OSS or Grafana Enterprise versions 11.6.0 through 12.4.1 with the SQL Expressions feature toggle enabled are at risk. The vulnerability requires authenticated access (any role — viewer, editor, or admin) and the presence of a compatible Grafana Enterprise plugin. Grafana is deployed by tens of thousands of organizations including major technology companies, financial institutions, healthcare providers, and critical infrastructure operators. Instances that have not been upgraded to patched versions (11.6.14+, 12.1.10+, 12.2.8+, 12.3.6+, 12.4.2+) remain exposed.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attacker obtains any form of Grafana account access — even a read-only viewer account is sufficient, which could be achieved via credential stuffing, phishing, or insider threat. Step 2: Attacker identifies that the target Grafana instance has SQL Expressions enabled and that a compatible Grafana Enterprise plugin (such as the Sqlyze SQL driver) is installed. Step 3: Attacker crafts a malicious SQL expression query that exploits Grafana's improper file path validation, specifying an arbitrary target path on the server filesystem outside the intended data sandbox. Step 4: Grafana processes the query and writes the attacker-controlled content to the filesystem — for example, overwriting a Sqlyze driver binary or injecting a malicious AWS data source configuration. Step 5: The maliciously written configuration or binary is loaded by Grafana, enabling arbitrary code execution on the host OS, ultimately allowing the attacker to add their SSH public key to the server's authorized_keys file and establish a persistent backdoor with full host access.
what_you_should_do: Upgrade all Grafana instances immediately to patched versions: 11.6.14, 12.1.10, 12.2.8, 12.3.6, 12.4.2, or 13.0.0
As an immediate interim measure before patching, disable the SQL Expressions feature toggle in grafana.ini by setting sqlExpressions = false in the [feature_toggles] section
Audit Grafana user accounts and remove or suspend any viewer accounts belonging to untrusted or former employees — viewer access is sufficient to exploit this vulnerability
Inventory installed Grafana Enterprise plugins, particularly the Sqlyze SQL driver, and temporarily disable any non-essential plugins that could be used in the exploit chain
Check the Grafana host filesystem for unexpected file modifications in plugin directories and inspect ~/.ssh/authorized_keys for unauthorized entries indicating possible compromise
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: Grafana is the monitoring backbone for most modern engineering and security operations teams — a compromised Grafana instance gives attackers not just control of one server, but deep insight into the entire organization's infrastructure health, and direct access to the credentials Grafana uses to connect to databases, Kubernetes clusters, cloud APIs, and other critical systems. The most dangerous aspect is the low prerequisite: any Grafana account holder, including contractors, vendors, or compromised former employees with view-only access, can trigger the full exploit chain. A successful attack could blind the security team by disabling their own monitoring while simultaneously using stolen data source credentials to pivot into production databases and cloud environments.

tactics.description: Attackers exploit the Grafana web interface's SQL Expressions feature with any valid low-privilege user account to initiate the file write exploit chain

tactics.technique_name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: Unix Shell
tactics.description: The exploit chain culminates in arbitrary OS command execution on the Grafana host via manipulated plugin drivers or configuration files
The exploit's final stage involves injecting the attacker's SSH public key into the server's authorized_keys file, establishing persistent backdoor access that survives Grafana restarts

tactics.technique_name: Masquerading: Match Legitimate Name or Location
tactics.description: Malicious files are written to Grafana's existing plugin and configuration directories, blending with legitimate application files to evade file integrity and EDR detection

With host-level access, attackers can read Grafana configuration files containing plaintext or weakly protected database passwords, cloud API keys, and service account tokens stored for data source connections
Extracted data source credentials enable lateral movement from the compromised Grafana host to connected databases, Kubernetes clusters, cloud accounts (AWS, GCP, Azure), and monitoring backends

detection_guidance.summary: Detection requires monitoring Grafana's SQL expression execution for unusual file path references, combined with host-level filesystem integrity monitoring on the Grafana server. The exploit leaves traces in Grafana's internal query logs (file path manipulation in SQL expressions) and on the filesystem (unexpected writes to plugin directories). Post-exploitation, look for outbound SSH connections from the Grafana host, which indicate successful backdoor installation. Grafana Enterprise audit logs provide the most detailed activity trail.

log_sources.description: Grafana's internal logs capture SQL expression executions including query content; file path references in SQL expression queries are strong exploitation indicators
Real-time filesystem monitoring on the Grafana host detects unauthorized writes to plugin directories and configuration files outside normal upgrade events
Grafana Enterprise's built-in audit logging captures all user actions including API calls, dashboard queries, and data transformation history with user attribution
Monitor for outbound SSH connections (port 22) originating from the Grafana server to external or unexpected IP addresses, indicating successful reverse shell or backdoor installation

what_to_look_for.indicator: SQL expression queries containing absolute file path references
what_to_look_for.description: SQL Expressions that reference absolute file system paths (e.g., '/etc/', '/home/', '/.ssh/', '/opt/grafana/') rather than database table or column names indicate file write exploitation attempts

what_to_look_for.indicator: Unexpected file modifications in Grafana plugin directory
what_to_look_for.description: New or recently modified files in Grafana plugin directories (especially Sqlyze driver binaries or .so files) that do not correspond to a scheduled Grafana upgrade or plugin installation event
what_to_look_for.log_field: auditd: type=SYSCALL AND syscall=openat AND flags=O_WRONLY AND path='/var/lib/grafana/plugins/*' AND uid!=grafana_service_uid

what_to_look_for.indicator: Modification of authorized_keys on Grafana host
what_to_look_for.description: Any write operations to SSH authorized_keys files on the Grafana server after initial deployment indicate potential SSH backdoor installation as the final exploit stage
what_to_look_for.log_field: auditd: type=SYSCALL AND syscall IN (open,openat,write) AND path MATCHES '.*\.ssh/authorized_keys' AND success=yes

what_to_look_for.indicator: Outbound SSH connection from Grafana host to external IP
what_to_look_for.description: SSH connections (port 22) originating from the Grafana server host to external IP addresses not in the approved administrator list indicate successful exploitation and backdoor callback
what_to_look_for.log_field: netflow: src_ip=[grafana_host_ip] AND dst_port=22 AND dst_ip NOT IN [admin_bastion_ips]

immediate_actions.action: Disable SQL Expressions feature toggle immediately as emergency interim measure
immediate_actions.description: Before patching is possible, disable the SQL Expressions feature in Grafana's configuration file (grafana.ini) or via environment variable. This removes the attack vector without requiring a full restart in most configurations. Validate the toggle is disabled by checking the Grafana feature toggles API.
immediate_actions.command:
# In grafana.ini under [feature_toggles]:
# sqlExpressions = false
# Or via environment variable:
# export GF_FEATURE_TOGGLES_ENABLE=$(echo $GF_FEATURE_TOGGLES_ENABLE | sed 's/sqlExpressions//')
# Verify: curl -s http://localhost:3000/api/frontend/settings | jq '.featureToggles.sqlExpressions'

immediate_actions.action: Upgrade all Grafana instances to patched versions
immediate_actions.description: Apply the security patches released on March 27, 2026 across all supported release lines. Target version depends on your current branch: 11.6.14+, 12.1.10+, 12.2.8+, 12.3.6+, 12.4.2+, or 13.0.0+. Enterprise customers should follow the enterprise upgrade path via Grafana's download portal.
immediate_actions.command:
# Docker: docker pull grafana/grafana:12.4.2
# Ubuntu/Debian: apt-get update && apt-get install grafana=12.4.2
# RPM: yum update grafana-12.4.2
# Verify: curl -s http://localhost:3000/api/health | jq .version

immediate_actions.action: Audit and restrict Grafana user accounts
immediate_actions.description: Review all Grafana user accounts. Suspend or remove viewer accounts belonging to former employees, contractors, or untrusted third parties. Viewer-level access is sufficient to trigger the exploit if SQL Expressions is enabled and Enterprise plugins are installed.
immediate_actions.command:
# List all users via API:
curl -H 'Authorization: Bearer <ADMIN_TOKEN>' http://localhost:3000/api/users?perpage=1000 | jq '.[] | {id, login, role, isDisabled}'
# Grafana CLI: grafana-cli admin reset-admin-password <newpassword>

immediate_actions.action: Inventory and disable non-essential Grafana Enterprise plugins
immediate_actions.description: List all installed Grafana Enterprise plugins, especially the Sqlyze SQL driver, which is used in the full exploit chain. Temporarily disable any non-essential Enterprise plugins until the Grafana instance is fully patched.
immediate_actions.command:
# List installed plugins:
ls /var/lib/grafana/plugins/
grafana-cli plugins list-remote --store
# Disable a plugin: mv /var/lib/grafana/plugins/sqlyze /var/lib/grafana/plugins/sqlyze.disabled

immediate_actions.action: Check Grafana host for indicators of compromise
immediate_actions.description: If patching was delayed and the vulnerability window was open, inspect the filesystem for evidence of exploitation: unexpected files in plugin directories, modifications to authorized_keys, or new cron jobs. If compromise is confirmed, rotate all credentials stored in Grafana data sources (databases, cloud APIs, etc.).
immediate_actions.command:
# Check for recent unauthorized file changes in plugin dirs:
find /var/lib/grafana/plugins -newer /var/log/grafana/grafana.log -type f -ls
# Check authorized_keys:
cat /root/.ssh/authorized_keys && cat /home/grafana/.ssh/authorized_keys 2>/dev/null
# Check for new cron entries:
crontab -l && ls /etc/cron.d/ | sort -n
